name: Envelopment

industry_framings.name: Healthcare ED triage at Meridian Health Network

central_tension: How tightly do you fence in an AI you can't fully explain?
background_copy: Some AI systems work well, but nobody — not even the people who built them — can fully explain why they decide what they decide. So how do you safely deploy something you can't see inside? The research community calls the answer envelopment. You don't make the AI transparent. You draw walls around it. You decide carefully what data it sees, what actions it's allowed to take, and who gets to overrule it. In this scenario, you're deploying an AI tool at a hospital that helps triage emergency patients. The model is accurate. It's also complex. Over one year, you choose how much to trust it, where to put humans in the loop, and how to react when it surprises you. Every quarter, pressure to scale up runs into pressure to stay safe. Your job is to figure out where you stand — and what trade-offs you can live with.

briefing_intro.where_you_start: You're the new AI Deployment Lead at Meridian Health, a three-hospital network. The board approved an AI ED triage tool six weeks ago. The model is accurate, but it's complex — nobody on your team can fully explain why it flags certain patients. The ED chief loves it. The COO is impatient about ROI. The regulator is watching. Now you have one year.
briefing_intro.what_you_decide: Every quarter, you'll face four kinds of choices. Defensive moves tighten what the AI sees and does — curating training data, adding human checkpoints, restricting where its outputs go. Aggressive moves loosen the gate to capture performance — expanding scope, raising autonomy, skipping audits. Monitoring moves keep your hand on the pulse — running audits, surveying users, briefing the regulator. Structural moves are long-term investments — reskilling the team, hiring a risk specialist, establishing review committees. You can't break every brick. You have to choose where to spend your year. The pattern of those choices will define both the deployment and the kind of manager you became.

anchor_sources.citation: Asatiani, A., Malo, P., Nagbol, P. R., Penttinen, E., Rinta-Kahila, T., & Salovaara, A. (2021). Sociotechnical Envelopment of AI: An Approach for Organisational Deployments of Inscrutable AI. Journal of the Association for Information Systems, 22(2), 325-352.
anchor_sources.skim_section: Section 3 (the field case) and Table 2 (the four methods)
anchor_sources.contribution: Studied a company that deployed an AI nobody could fully explain. Coined the term 'envelopment' — fencing in the AI's inputs, outputs, and what it can touch — as a way to deploy it safely anyway.

anchor_sources.citation: Dux, N., et al. (2025). Governance by Design: Translating AI Risk into Organisational Architecture. arXiv preprint.
anchor_sources.contribution: Updates envelopment for agentic AI (the kind that takes actions on its own). Argues governance has to be built into how the system is designed, not bolted on later.

anchor_sources.citation: Li, Y., et al. (2026). Conformal Risk Control for AI Deployments. ACM TOIS.
anchor_sources.contribution: Shows a real example of how AI in healthcare can quietly miss serious cases. The point: surface-level performance metrics can hide problems that only a deeper audit reveals.

anchor_sources.citation: Barocas, S., & Selbst, A. D. (2016). Big Data's Disparate Impact. California Law Review, 104, 671-732.
anchor_sources.contribution: The foundational legal paper on AI bias. Explains how unfairness gets baked into AI systems unintentionally — through data, labels, or hidden proxies — and why existing law often can't catch it.

contributing_sources.citation: National Institute of Standards and Technology (2023). AI Risk Management Framework 1.0 (NIST AI 100-1).
contributing_sources.contribution: The US government's official framework for managing AI risk — Govern, Map, Measure, Manage.

contributing_sources.citation: Yu, K., et al. (2025). Trust Calibration Under Model Opacity. AI & Society.
contributing_sources.contribution: Found that people consistently overestimate how much AI is helping them. Relevant because trust without calibration is dangerous when you can't see inside the model.

bricks.label: Curate data
bricks.briefing_description: Tightens what the model sees during training, raising boundedness meaningfully. Modest hit to performance.
bricks.research_note: One of the four envelopment methods Asatiani et al. (2021) document in their field case.

bricks.label: Add checkpoint
bricks.briefing_description: Inserts a human review step before high-stakes outputs. Boundedness up, performance slightly down, trust up.
bricks.research_note: Human-oversight envelopment method from Asatiani et al. (2021); also a NIST AI RMF Govern function.

bricks.label: Restrict output
bricks.briefing_description: Constrains the model's allowed responses to a vetted shortlist. Strong boundedness lift; performance takes a real hit.
bricks.research_note: Output-restriction envelopment from Asatiani et al. (2021).

bricks.label: Document limits
bricks.briefing_description: Publishes the system's known limits and failure modes. Modest boundedness, real trust lift.
bricks.research_note: Documentation as envelopment surface — Asatiani et al. (2021) Section 4; reinforced by NIST AI RMF Map function.

bricks.label: Choose simpler model
bricks.briefing_description: Swaps in a less expressive, more inspectable model. Large boundedness lift; substantial performance cost.
bricks.research_note: Model-simplification as envelopment alternative — Asatiani et al. (2021) discussion; also Yu et al. (2025) on inscrutability tradeoffs.

bricks.label: Set escalation protocol
bricks.briefing_description: Codifies when and how a case must escalate to a human. Boundedness and trust both gain.
bricks.research_note: Escalation as a governance-by-design pattern — Dux et al. (2025) Section 4.

bricks.label: Limit deploy hours
bricks.briefing_description: Runs the model only during staffed hours. Boundedness up, performance down by the share of the day you blocked.
bricks.research_note: Environmental constraint envelopment — Asatiani et al. (2021).

bricks.label: Tighten data scope
bricks.briefing_description: Narrows the inputs the model is allowed to consume. Boundedness and a small risk reduction; performance gives.
bricks.research_note: Asatiani et al. (2021) training-data curation; Dux et al. (2025) governance-by-design.

bricks.label: Loosen gate
bricks.briefing_description: Relaxes a previously strict approval gate. Performance gains; boundedness loses a real amount.
bricks.research_note: Inversion of an Asatiani et al. (2021) envelopment method, used to illustrate the cost side of bounding.

bricks.label: Expand scope
bricks.briefing_description: Authorises the model to handle additional case types. Performance up; both boundedness and risk move against you.
bricks.research_note: Scope expansion as a known anti-envelopment move — Asatiani et al. (2021); risk amplification documented in Barocas & Selbst (2016).

bricks.label: Increase autonomy
bricks.briefing_description: Lets the model act without human confirmation on a wider class of cases. Big performance lift; biggest hit to boundedness and risk.
bricks.research_note: Removing the human-oversight envelopment method (Asatiani et al. 2021); risk amplification per Barocas & Selbst (2016).

bricks.label: Skip audit
bricks.briefing_description: Defers a planned external audit. Small performance bump from saved cycles; risk grows.
bricks.research_note: Inversion of monitoring envelopment, and a known precursor to Q3 incidents — Barocas & Selbst (2016); NIST AI RMF.

bricks.label: Push to 3rd hospital
bricks.briefing_description: Deploys to a third site before the second is fully stabilised. Performance and risk both gain; internal trust suffers.
bricks.research_note: Composite — premature scale is documented as a deployment failure pattern across Dux et al. (2025) and Asatiani et al. (2021).

bricks.label: Auto-approve low-risk
bricks.briefing_description: Removes the human review step for cases the model rates low-risk. Performance up; boundedness down.
bricks.research_note: Inversion of Asatiani et al. (2021) human-oversight method.

bricks.label: Run conformal audit
bricks.briefing_description: Schedules a statistical audit using conformal-prediction methods. Risk down, trust up, modest performance cost.
bricks.research_note: Operationalises Li et al. (2026) conformal-risk-control framework; aligns with NIST AI RMF Measure function.

bricks.label: Survey users
bricks.briefing_description: Collects structured user feedback on the model's outputs. Trust gains; small performance cost.
bricks.research_note: Stakeholder feedback as monitoring — NIST AI RMF Manage function.

bricks.label: Build risk dashboard
bricks.briefing_description: Stands up a dashboard tracking model-error rates by population. Risk down; effort cost shows up as performance loss.
bricks.research_note: NIST AI RMF Measure function operationalised; Li et al. (2026) on visible risk metrics.

bricks.label: Track overrides
bricks.briefing_description: Instruments human overrides of the model and reviews them weekly. Small but cheap risk and trust gains.
bricks.research_note: NIST AI RMF Measure pattern; aligns with Asatiani et al. (2021) human-oversight method.

bricks.label: Brief regulator
bricks.briefing_description: Schedules a pre-emptive briefing with the relevant regulator. Trust gains; modest performance cost.
bricks.research_note: Regulatory engagement as envelopment surface — Barocas & Selbst (2016); Dux et al. (2025) governance-by-design.

bricks.label: Publish accuracy
bricks.briefing_description: Publishes the model's accuracy by subpopulation. Big trust lift; slight boundedness and performance cost.
bricks.research_note: Transparency as monitoring — NIST AI RMF Map function; Asatiani et al. (2021) documentation surface.

bricks.label: Reskill team
bricks.briefing_description: Funds a clinical-AI literacy program for staff. Big trust gain. Takes two paddle hits.
bricks.research_note: Capacity-building as governance — Dux et al. (2025) Section 4.

bricks.label: Hire risk specialist
bricks.briefing_description: Adds a dedicated AI-risk role to the team. Risk and trust both move favourably. Two hits.
bricks.research_note: Standing-role institutionalisation — Dux et al. (2025); NIST AI RMF Govern.

bricks.label: Establish review committee
bricks.briefing_description: Convenes a standing cross-functional AI review committee. Trust gain, risk reduction. Two hits.
bricks.research_note: Committee-based envelopment — Dux et al. (2025) governance-by-design.

bricks.label: Create explainer role
bricks.briefing_description: Adds a clinician-facing AI-explainer role. Trust up, small risk reduction. Two hits.
bricks.research_note: Boundary-spanner pattern — Dux et al. (2025); Yu et al. (2025) on trust calibration under opacity.

bricks.label: Emergency audit
bricks.briefing_description: Halts and inspects. Massive risk reduction, real performance cost, trust gain. Two hits. Only available in Q3, only if you've already lost ground.
bricks.research_note: Recovery surface — Li et al. (2026) conformal-risk-control under detected drift; Dux et al. (2025) crisis-mode envelopment.

1.label: Q1 · ED OVERRIDE
1.text: An ED physician overrode the AI's safety flag at 3am. No patient harm. The chief of staff wants a written explanation by Friday.

1.label: Q1 · COO MEMO
1.text: The COO's memo: "We bought an AI. We deployed an interpreter. Help me understand what we've shipped." Board notes it.

1.label: Q1 · SOLID START
1.text: Operations team posts a green dashboard. ED leadership defends the program in a department meeting.

2.label: Q2 · DRIFT
2.text: Override rate climbs. Clinicians are routing around the model rather than fighting it. Performance numbers stay up — for now.

2.label: Q2 · TIGHTENING
2.text: Risk team sees the leading indicators and tightens the gates further. Performance drops. Clinicians notice the trust gesture.

2.label: Q2 · MIXED SIGNALS
2.text: Risk is climbing but the response is incoherent — some tightening, some loosening. Performance suffers. Risk drops slightly.

2.label: Q2 · STAFF RESISTANCE
2.text: An ED resident files a formal complaint about being asked to override their judgment. Internal trust collapses further.

2.label: Q2 · OPERATIONS NORMAL
2.text: No fires. Steady volume. The risk team's weekly memo notes "no novel signals."

3.label: Q3 · MISDIAGNOSIS
3.text: A misdiagnosis becomes public. Patient unharmed but family is angry. Local TV picks it up. The chief of staff stops returning your calls.

3.label: Q3 · REGULATOR ESCALATES
3.text: State health department asks for a Friday meeting. Your counsel wants the AI committee chair on the call. The bounds you removed look very visible from the outside.

3.label: Q3 · REGULATOR CHECK-IN
3.text: State health department schedules a routine check-in. You volunteer a performance pull-back as a gesture. Trust recovers a step.

3.label: Q3 · BREAKTHROUGH
3.text: Quarterly review: ED chief and risk lead jointly present results. Board confidence ticks up. The model becomes the program — not just a tool.

3.label: Q3 · HOLDING
3.text: Volume and override rates unremarkable. The board meeting is uneventful, which is itself notable.

quarter_wrap_rules.label: BUSINESS CREDIBILITY
quarter_wrap_rules.text: Two consecutive quarters of weak performance numbers. The board's finance committee wants a viability review.

quarter_wrap_rules.label: BOARD CONFIDENCE COLLAPSE
quarter_wrap_rules.text: An emergency board call. You spend the call defending the program rather than running it.

options.label: Frame as success — push for scale
options.description: Recommend doubling the deployment footprint in Year 2. Aggressive growth narrative.

options.label: Frame as solid foundation
options.description: Recommend continuing at current footprint with no expansion. Hold-and-improve narrative.

options.label: Frame as cautionary — pull back
options.description: Recommend narrowing the deployment scope while you stabilise. Defensive narrative.

regulator_escalation.label: Q4 · REGULATOR ESCALATION
regulator_escalation.text: The contested regulator follow-up arrives. Your counsel needs you on the call. One life is the cost of the meeting itself.

archetypes.label: The Drifter
archetypes.reveal_text: You played, but you barely played. The model deployed itself. Whatever happened, you watched it happen.

archetypes.label: The Over-Bounded
archetypes.reveal_text: You built bounds so tight nothing got through them — including the value the AI was supposed to deliver. The team trusts the boundaries, not the tool. The board is asking why.

archetypes.label: The Guardian
archetypes.reveal_text: You built a model people trust. Whether you built one they'll fund is the question Year Two will answer. The ED chief will defend you to the board. The COO will not.

archetypes.label: The Cowboy
archetypes.reveal_text: You pushed performance and the model delivered — until it didn't. The incidents you collected are the price of the velocity you celebrated. The numbers are real. So is the body count of credibility.

archetypes.label: The Risk-Taker
archetypes.reveal_text: You took the bet. You loosened bounds where the value was. The performance numbers reward you. Whether you'll get to take the same bet twice depends on what the regulator thinks of your luck.

archetypes.label: The Compliance Officer
archetypes.reveal_text: You read the incident reports and tightened. Your bounds moved with the evidence. You're not the visionary the board wanted at hiring. You're the one they want now.

archetypes.label: The Pragmatist
archetypes.trigger: def_pct >= 0.2 AND def_pct <= 0.55 AND agg_pct >= 0.15 AND agg_pct <= 0.55 AND mon_pct >= 0.2
archetypes.reveal_text: You did not pick a philosophy. You picked moments. Tighten here, loosen there, watch everywhere. The model survived. So did you.

archetypes.label: The Drifter
archetypes.reveal_text: Your choices didn't add up to a posture. The model deployed; the year passed; the pattern you left is the absence of one. There's a manager Year Two needs. You're choosing now whether to be them.
